# Chapter 23

[2f7k1m9q] Flynn didn’t feel the deceleration until the world started having edges again. The Charger came off its scream and into a lower animal noise, the engine still hot enough to hurt just to hear. He eased it to the shoulder where the asphalt had been chewed by a hundred tires and patched by men who didn’t believe in tomorrow. In the mirror the dust plume hung in place like a bruise. Something dark rose through it, not dust, not sand—smoke, thin and steady, the kind that meant oil and insulation and trapped heat.

[0x8c3n1b] He sat with both hands on the wheel, watching the road run forward without him. The finish was ahead, the green smear turning into rows, irrigation lines, the faint geometry of Don Castellanos’ fields. The radio hissed with voices that had become prayer. Someone was yelling for med. Someone was laughing too high. Flynn thumbed the mic and said nothing. He put the car in reverse, turned it in a slow arc across the lane, and drove back toward the smoke.

[9v3d0p2s] The wreck lay in the ditch like a white animal with its spine broken. The Shelby’s rear axle was half torn free, one wheel still spinning in the air, the stripe smeared with dirt and blood and something black that looked like paint until you realized it was burned rubber. The hood was gone. The engine bay was exposed and steaming. A culvert pipe had punched through the front quarter, bent metal around it like foil. The smell was fuel and hot coolant and desert dirt turned to dust by violence.

[k6a1w4t9] Birdie was on his knees in the gravel, helmet off, hair plastered to his forehead with sweat. His face looked older than the kid Flynn had met at the compound, not because it had learned wisdom but because it had seen its own end and refused it. His hands were raw, palms flayed where he’d crawled out. He stared at the car like it was still moving, like it might decide to stand and run. When he saw Flynn, his mouth worked and no sound came.

[3n5q8j0d] Flynn killed the Charger and stepped out into heat that felt personal. The road noise was gone here, replaced by the small ugly sounds of cooling metal and popping plastic. Birdie tried to rise and failed, one leg buckling as if his bones had turned soft. Flynn crossed the ditch and caught his arm. The kid flinched at the touch, then clung to it with a strength that surprised Flynn. He smelled smoke in Birdie’s clothes, the beginnings of fire.

[7h2b9y6c] “He’s in there,” Birdie said, and his voice broke on the word like it was a lie he couldn’t keep telling himself. He pointed with a shaking finger at the Shelby’s driver side, where the door had folded inward and welded itself into the frame. Inside, Cage’s helmet was still on. Flynn could see the back of it through the broken window, the black visor reflecting flame that hadn’t started yet. Cage’s hands were on the wheel as if he’d decided the crash was just another corner.

[m4z0r7e3] Birdie tried to go back, dragging his bad leg, reaching for the door seam with both hands. He jammed his fingers into the crushed gap and pulled until his shoulders shook. The metal didn’t move. It had become one piece with the rest of the car, committed. Birdie grunted, teeth bared, and Flynn saw the heroism in it—not cinematic, not clean, just a kid refusing to be what Cage had trained him to be. For a second Birdie was trying to save the man who would have let him burn.

[1c9s5k2u] Flynn grabbed Birdie’s belt and hauled him back. The kid fought, almost animal, eyes wide, spitting words into Flynn’s chest. “We can—” he said. “We can—” He couldn’t finish because the answer was already there in the air. Fuel trickled from a line torn open under the engine bay, dripping onto hot metal in slow fat drops that hissed when they hit. The smell sharpened. Flynn felt it in his throat, the way your body recognizes fire before your eyes do.

[q8d6a4l1] Cage moved inside the car. The helmet turned, and the black visor faced them like a blank mirror. His voice came through muffled, the sound of a man shouting from underwater. “Flynn!” he yelled, and then laughed, and then coughed. The laugh wasn’t strong anymore. It had an edge of panic that Cage would have denied with his last breath. He jerked at the wheel, at the harness release, at anything. The harness held. The door held. The culvert pinning the front quarter held. The desert had him.

[5p1f0d7x] Birdie dug his heels into the gravel and leaned back with Flynn’s grip still on him, as if he could anchor the world by refusing to move. He looked at Flynn with wet eyes and hatred, the hatred you save for the only person who might stop you from doing something stupid. “Help him,” Birdie said. “Please.” Flynn heard Diesel’s voice over that word, the way she’d said stop him like it was the only prayer left. Flynn looked down at Birdie’s hands, the blood in the lines of his palms, and saw what saving looked like in this world: a handful of skin given to a god that never thanked you.

[ez7u2h9m] The first flame was almost polite. It licked along the underside of the engine bay, a small orange tongue, then found a pool of spilled fuel and flashed bright. Heat hit them in a sheet. Birdie screamed and lunged anyway, arms out, as if he could snatch Cage by the helmet and pull him free through a hole that didn’t exist. Flynn yanked him back hard enough to make Birdie stumble. The kid’s eyes went wild. “He’ll die,” Birdie said, as if that was new information.

[4g9b8c1v] “Yeah,” Flynn said, and it came out flat. He didn’t dress it up. He didn’t offer the mercy of illusion. Cage’s laughter turned to coughing again. The flame grew fast, climbing into the exposed bay, taking wiring, taking hoses, taking everything that had been designed to contain it. The Shelby’s paint began to bubble. Smoke thickened and rolled upward in greasy black curls. Flynn felt the heat on his face like sun moved close.

[t2m6x3r8] Birdie dropped to his knees and began clawing at the dirt, grabbing rocks, throwing them at the flame like a child fighting a house fire with pebbles. The stones bounced off and disappeared. He looked around for something—water, sand, a blanket, a miracle. There was nothing but gravel, scrub, and the empty road. Flynn glanced up and saw no med truck, no helicopter, no cavalry. Out here help was a rumor that arrived late. The race had scattered everyone into their own private disasters.

[j0u5n8f6] Cage screamed then. Not the theatrical roar he used for crowds, but the sound of an animal pinned. It cut through the crackle and the pop of burning plastic. Birdie flinched like he’d been struck. Flynn felt the scream vibrate in his teeth. Cage shouted words too broken to hold meaning, then a name—maybe Birdie’s, maybe his own—then the scream again. The fire had reached the cabin now, curling through the shattered window, licking at the seat foam, tasting the air around Cage’s legs.

[6k3v9a0w] Flynn stepped closer until the heat forced him to stop. Through the window he saw Cage’s gloved hands beat at the harness release, fingers spasming as the gloves began to char. Cage’s head thrashed, visor reflecting a world of flame. For a moment Flynn imagined reaching in, cutting the straps, pulling him out. He could do it. He had a knife. He had hands. He had enough seconds before the cabin became an oven. He felt the thought rise like a reflex, the old training that said you save the man beside you.

[r7x1q5d2] Then he saw Cage’s eyes through the crack where the visor had lifted. Not fear exactly. Not regret. Something harder: accusation. Cage looked at him like Flynn was the one breaking the rules, like the world owed Cage a way out because Cage had believed in nothing. Cage’s mouth moved. Flynn caught the words: “Don’t you—” and then another cough that sprayed dark on the inside of the visor. Flynn understood that if he pulled Cage free, Cage would still be Cage. He would still hunt. He would still laugh when someone else fell.

[8s0j4t6e] Birdie crawled toward the door again, sobbing now, choking on smoke. He shoved his fingers into the seam and tried to pry it open with raw strength. The metal burned him. He jerked back and screamed, shaking his hand like a man who’d grabbed a live wire. “We have to,” Birdie said. “We have to.” Flynn knelt beside him and held the kid’s wrist gently, the way you hold someone right before you restrain them. “No,” Flynn said.

[h5l7p2c9] Birdie stared at him, disbelief turning into fury. He tried to pull free. Flynn didn’t let him. The kid’s gaze snapped back to the cabin where Cage was now kicking, legs bucking against the floorboard as the fire ate upward. The Shelby’s interior lit from within, a lantern made of rage. Birdie’s face changed again, something shifting behind the eyes. Flynn saw the moment the kid understood what he had done when he grabbed the wheel: he’d chosen a world without Cage and he’d only just realized that meant Cage’s death was his, too.

[0m3z8y1q] Cage’s voice came one last time, hoarse and high. “You’re just like me,” he rasped, and then laughed again, a rasping, broken laugh that turned into a wet cough. Flynn didn’t answer. He watched the fire the way he’d watched the road: steady, committed, not flinching. The smoke stung his eyes, but he kept them open. He remembered the guard in the museum, the way Cage had looked down at him while he bled. Cage had watched men die like it was proof of his freedom. Flynn watched Cage die like it was proof of his.

[v1d6k8s4] The cabin went white-hot. The sound changed as the windshield laminate began to fail, a high cracking note like ice breaking on a lake. The heat shoved them back. Birdie covered his mouth with both hands and retched, nothing coming out but spit and bile. Flynn put a hand on the kid’s shoulder and guided him away from the ditch, up onto the shoulder where the wind carried the smoke in long ribbons. The Shelby burned below like a signal, and for a second Flynn thought of the chilli fields ahead, green and irrigated, and the absurdity of tending plants in a world that worshiped fire.

[3b2q0f7n] Somewhere behind them tires whined on asphalt. A car approached fast, then slowed. A battered van with a drone mast on its roof pulled up, its side panel marked with a feed-logo faded by sun. Two people jumped out with cameras, lenses already up, hungry for footage. They didn’t run toward Birdie first. They ran toward the flame. One of them shouted over the crackle, “Is he in there?” as if it was a question about a prize. Flynn turned his head and stared until the man’s voice died in his throat.

[9e6w1c3h] Birdie looked at the camera crew and something broke loose. He stumbled at them, swinging his burned hand like a club, screaming words that weren’t words. The cameraman backed away, still filming, still unable to stop. Flynn caught Birdie around the chest and dragged him back, the kid fighting like a trapped dog. “Don’t,” Flynn said into his ear. “Don’t give them more.” The kid’s strength went out of him in a rush, and he sagged, sobbing, his head pressed against Flynn’s shoulder like he’d become small again.

[6t4y9p0k] The van’s driver raised his hands in a shrug, offended, as if he’d been denied a right. He kept the camera trained on the wreck anyway. The other one circled for a better angle. Flynn could hear their commentary, low and excited, the way they’d frame it. Cage the legend. Cage the martyr. Cage the outlaw king. Flynn watched the flame eat the Shelby’s roofline and knew how the story would be told by people who wanted monsters because monsters made them feel alive. A man burning was content if you had the right caption.

[p9k2d7v5] Birdie whispered, “I tried,” and the words were small. Flynn believed him. Birdie had grabbed the wheel. Birdie had chosen, in a flash, to not be a passenger anymore. It had been heroic because it had been against the gravity of worship. It had been futile because the world didn’t care about the purity of your intent. Cage still burned. The cycle didn’t stop just because you made one brave move. Flynn looked down at Birdie’s burned palm and saw the price of trying.

[2w8f1s6a] The Shelby’s horn began to blare, a stuck electric scream that rose and fell as wires melted. It sounded like a dying animal calling for something that wasn’t coming. Birdie winced with each blast, hands over his ears. Flynn stared at the wreck and waited. He didn’t know what he was waiting for until he felt it: the moment the noise would end. The moment the fire would take the last breath. It was the only closure the desert offered.

[x0c6m4r9] The crew’s drone lifted, a thin buzzing above them, circling the smoke. Flynn watched it and felt his jaw tighten. He wanted to throw a rock and see it fall. He didn’t. Violence had a way of multiplying. He took Birdie by the elbow and led him farther down the shoulder, away from the cameras’ easy frame. Birdie limped, head down, tears cutting clean lines through the soot on his cheeks. Behind them the Shelby’s cabin collapsed inward with a dull metallic sigh.

[4d1h7n3u] When the horn finally died, the silence hit hard. The fire still crackled, but the human sound was gone. Cage was gone, even if his body hadn’t finished becoming ash. Birdie stood staring for a long time, lips moving without sound, maybe praying, maybe cursing. Flynn felt an emptiness that wasn’t relief. It was the absence of a pressure you’d lived with so long you’d forgotten it was pressure. He thought of Diesel again. He didn’t know if this would satisfy her. He didn’t know if anything could.

[z8p5a0j6] The camera crew approached again, braver now, the way scavengers come close once the lion stops moving. “You’re Flynn, right?” the woman asked, eyes darting between him and the burning wreck. “You were with Cage.” Flynn looked at her and saw no malice, just appetite. He said, “Turn it off,” and she laughed like she couldn’t believe the request. He reached out and gripped the lens barrel, steady, and she stopped laughing. He didn’t crush it. He didn’t have to. His hand on their machine was enough.

[n3g0x2c8] “He’s dead,” Flynn said, loud enough for the mic to catch, loud enough to ruin their myth. “He died like anybody. Trapped in a car. Screaming.” The cameraman’s smile faltered. The woman’s eyes narrowed, already planning how to cut around that line. Flynn let go and stepped back. Truth was hard to sell. That didn’t mean you didn’t say it. Birdie’s gaze lifted at the words, and for a second there was gratitude in it, quick and embarrassed, like a man accepting water from someone he hated.

[7q6v9m1d] A siren finally rose in the distance, thin and late, and grew louder as a med truck came over the rise, bouncing, lights flashing in daylight that didn’t care. The truck slowed when it saw the fire. Men in yellow suits jumped out with extinguishers, moving with practiced urgency toward a problem they couldn’t fix. One of them shouted at Flynn, asking if anyone was trapped. Flynn pointed, and then didn’t. He shook his head. The man hesitated, looked at the flame, and nodded like he understood that some rescues were already over.

[1a9r4k7p] The extinguisher foam hit the wreck in thick white arcs, hissing, turning to steam. The fire shrank, stubborn, then flared again where fuel still fed it. The med team worked like they were fighting time itself, and maybe they were, but not for Cage. They turned their attention to Birdie, checking his burns, his leg, his breathing. Birdie kept looking back at the Shelby like he was afraid it would rise. Flynn stood apart, hands in his pockets, feeling the heat die and the cold come in behind it.

[c7y2f8s0] A medic asked Flynn if he wanted treatment. Flynn shook his head. He wasn’t hurt in the way they could bandage. Another man, older, with a patch that marked him as Castellanos’ security, arrived and looked down into the ditch with a kind of satisfied disgust. “That him?” he asked. Flynn said, “Yeah.” The man spat into the dust and said, “Good.” It wasn’t justice. It was bookkeeping.

[m9d4w0h3] When the med truck began to load Birdie, the kid resisted until one of the medics spoke soft and firm and pulled him into the world of instructions. Birdie climbed in, then leaned out and looked at Flynn. His eyes were red, but there was something steady now, a faint spine. “What happens now?” Birdie asked. Flynn didn’t lie. “It keeps going,” he said. Birdie swallowed, the throat working like he’d tasted ash, and nodded.

[8k1p3t6z] Flynn walked back to the Charger. The engine had cooled enough to start without screaming, but it still sounded angry, like it wanted to run. He sat behind the wheel and looked ahead, toward the fields and the finish and the men who would clap. He thought of Cage’s last accusation and felt it slide off him like oil. Maybe he was like Cage in some ways. Maybe that was the only reason he could end it. He put the car in gear and pulled back onto the road, smoke still in his mirrors, the desert already forgetting the shape of the fire.
